title: Punjabi Sarson Saag

ingredients.name: fresh spinach leaves, washed and roughly chopped
visual_checks: substitute for mustard greens — use if sarson unavailable
ingredients.notes: substitute for mustard greens — use if sarson unavailable

subs.name: if you can find actual sarson (mustard greens), use 300 g sarson + 100 g spinach for authentic bitter-pungent flavour

ingredients.name: fresh fenugreek leaves (methi), stripped from stems
cornflour (makki atta substitute for thickening)
visual_checks: traditionally makki atta (coarse corn flour); cornflour works as a substitute
ingredients.notes: traditionally makki atta (coarse corn flour); cornflour works as a substitute

subs.name: 2 tbsp wheat flour (atta) works similarly as a thickener; add in the same step
ghee entirely if you prefer; the white butter is the traditional garnish but ghee is a classic Punjabi choice too
oil for a less rich tempering; no significant impact on the final flavour

steps.text: Pressure cook the spinach, fenugreek leaves, tomatoes, half the ginger, 2 cloves of garlic, green chillies and salt with half a cup of water for 2 whistles on medium flame. Allow the pressure to release naturally.

Drain any excess liquid and coarsely blend or mash the cooked greens using a blender or the back of a heavy ladle. Sarson saag should be textured, not completely smooth — stop blending while some fibrous texture remains.

Return the mashed greens to the pot on low flame. Stir in the cornflour and cook for 5 minutes, stirring constantly, until the saag thickens. The cornflour thickens the dish and removes any raw grassiness.

Heat ghee in a small pan over medium flame. Add cumin seeds; let them crackle. Add the remaining garlic and ginger and fry until golden and fragrant, about 2 minutes. Add the onion and cook until deep golden brown, about 8 minutes.

Add red chilli powder to the tempering, stir for 10 seconds, then pour the entire tempering into the saag. Mix well and simmer on low flame for 10 minutes, stirring often so it does not catch at the bottom. Add garam masala in the last minute.

Taste for salt. Add the butter in pieces right before serving — do not stir it in completely; let pools of butter appear on top as a garnish. Serve in bowls with cornmeal roti (makki di roti), jaggery and a radish salad.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: spinach → if you can find actual sarson (mustard greens), use 300 g sarson + 100 g spinach for authentic bitter-pungent flavour; cornflour → 2 tbsp wheat flour (atta) works similarly as a thickener; add in the same step; butter → ghee entirely if you prefer; the white butter is the traditional garnish but ghee is a classic Punjabi choice too; ghee → oil for a less rich tempering; no significant impact on the final flavour
